title: Compliance Map Methodology
lede: How the Cross-Border RWA Compliance Map is researched, structured, and maintained.

s1.title: 1. Purpose
s1.p1: The Cross-Border RWA Compliance Map provides a concise, jurisdiction-by-issue overview of the regulatory landscape for tokenized real-world assets (RWAs) and stablecoins. It is designed to give practitioners and researchers a navigable starting point for cross-jurisdictional analysis — not a substitute for legal advice.
s1.p2: The matrix covers five jurisdictions: Hong Kong SAR (HK), Mainland China (CN), Singapore (SG), the United States (US), and the European Union (EU). It tracks five issue categories: RWA Issuance, Stablecoin Issuance, VASP / Exchange Licensing, Cross-Border Distribution, and Retail Access.

s2.title: 2. Signal Definitions
s2.intro: Each cell in the matrix carries one of four signals:

open.meaning: A clear regulatory pathway exists; requirements are defined and achievable.
open.typical: Licensing regime published; regulator has approved at least one entity.

conditional.meaning: A pathway exists but is subject to material conditions, pending guidance, or requires case-by-case regulatory engagement.
conditional.typical: Transitional provisions in effect; licensing regime enacted but no licences yet issued; sandbox or no-action letter required.

restricted.meaning: The activity is prohibited or practically unavailable to most participants.
restricted.typical: Statutory ban; no licensing regime exists; regulator has issued blanket prohibition notices.

placeholder.meaning: This cell has not yet been researched.
placeholder.typical: No information displayed; cell navigates to a placeholder page.

s2.footnote: Signals reflect the state of the primary regulatory framework at the date of last review. They do not account for fact-specific circumstances, contractual structures, or ongoing regulatory proceedings.

s3.title: 3. Research Process
s3.intro: Each cell is researched against the following source hierarchy:

statutes.heading: Primary statutes
statutes.body: — the text of the relevant ordinance, act, or regulation as published by the official legislative body.

guidance.heading: Regulator guidance
guidance.body: — supervisory policy manuals, consultation papers, circulars, and FAQs issued by the named regulator.

registers.heading: Regulator registers
registers.body: — public lists of licensed, registered, or sanctioned entities maintained by the regulator.

statements.heading: Official statements
statements.body: — speeches, press releases, and testimony by senior officials of the named regulator.

court.heading: Court records
court.body: — published judicial decisions interpreting the relevant statute.

media.heading: Major media / industry media
media.body: — used only to corroborate or provide context; never as a sole source for a signal determination.

s4.title: 4. Update Policy
s4.p1: Each cell records a last_reviewed date. Cells are prioritised for re-review when:
triggers: A relevant statute or regulation is amended or enacted.
The named regulator publishes new guidance, a licensing decision, or a prohibition notice.
A court publishes a decision that materially re-characterises the legal position.
Six months have elapsed since the last review.
s4.p2: The matrix version number (matrix_version) is incremented with each substantive change to any cell. Minor corrections (e.g., typographical fixes) do not increment the version.

s5.title: 5. Limitations
items: The matrix covers jurisdiction-level frameworks. Sub-national, state-level, or sectoral variations (e.g., US state money-transmitter licensing) are noted in practitioner notes where material but are not captured as separate rows.

Signals reflect the primary regulatory characterisation of the activity. A single real-world transaction may attract multiple regulatory overlays (e.g., securities law AND AML requirements) — each overlay is addressed in the relevant issue row, but the interaction between rows is the practitioner's responsibility to analyse.

Cells in "Conditional" status cover a wide range of practical difficulty. A cell showing Conditional in one jurisdiction may be trivially achievable; in another it may require multi-year regulatory engagement. Read the cell narrative carefully.
This matrix does not cover tax treatment, accounting standards, or private-law enforceability of smart contracts.

s6.title: 6. Corrections
s6.body: To report an error or outdated signal, contact research@rwa-index.com with the subject line "Compliance Map — [jurisdiction]-[issue]" and a supporting primary source. We aim to acknowledge within 5 business days.

disclaimer: This matrix is published for educational and research purposes only. It does not constitute legal advice, regulatory opinion, or a legal opinion in any jurisdiction. RWA-Index Research is not a law firm and does not provide legal services. Practitioners must obtain qualified legal advice before structuring any transaction.
